common_name: Alleppey Turmeric (High-Curcumin Kerala Turmeric for Gulf South Gardens)

planting.note: Plant rhizome pieces (2–3 inches with at least one bud) 2–4 inches deep after the last frost in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; harvest rhizomes in November–December after the leaves die back naturally — the die-back signals the completion of rhizome development.

directions: Plant rhizome pieces 2–4 inches deep after the last frost in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Alleppey turmeric is a high-curcumin selection named for the Alleppey (Alappuzha) region of Kerala, India — one of the primary commercial turmeric production areas — where this variety is selected for the deep orange-yellow color and high curcumin content that make it particularly valuable for culinary and medicinal uses. Curcumin is the bioactive compound responsible for turmeric's anti-inflammatory properties and vivid yellow-orange color; Alleppey selections typically contain 5–7% curcumin by dry weight versus 2–3% in ordinary turmeric. The turmeric plant itself is ornamentally beautiful in Gulf South tropical gardens: broad, paddle-shaped leaves (similar to canna) on upright stems reaching 3–4 feet, with occasional cream-and-pale-purple flower spikes that emerge from the leaf bases in summer. In zone 9, turmeric grows as a perennial if mulched heavily during winter, or the rhizomes can be stored frost-free and replanted each spring. Harvest fresh turmeric rhizomes in fall for immediate use — the fresh rhizome flavor is brighter and more complex than dried powder — or cure, dry, and powder for long-term storage. The plant grows best in the same conditions as its ginger relatives: warm, humid, partially shaded, with consistent moisture.

common_name: Lesser Galangal (Small Thai Cooking Galangal Root Herb)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; lesser galangal is smaller and more compact than greater galangal (A. galanga) and appropriate for container growing — a useful option for zone 9 gardeners who want to move the plant indoors during hard freezes.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Lesser Galangal (Alpinia officinarum) is a rhizomatous herb from southern China and Southeast Asia grown for its aromatic rhizomes, which provide the distinctive peppery-gingery-citrusy flavor that is essential to Thai tom kha soup, Thai curries, Indonesian laksa, and numerous other Southeast Asian dishes. The flavor differs significantly from ginger — sharper, more peppery, with pine and citrus overtones — and from greater galangal (A. galanga) which is milder and more floral. The smaller stature of lesser galangal (2–3 feet) compared to the 5–6 foot greater galangal makes it more manageable in typical garden settings and suitable for large containers. The rhizomes can be harvested from the edge of established clumps after the first year without disturbing the entire plant — a sustained harvest approach that allows the clump to continue producing indefinitely. In Gulf South gardens, lesser galangal thrives in the warm, humid conditions similar to those of its native growing range. The ornamental narrow leaves and occasional small white-and-red flower spikes provide year-round interest as a foliage plant in partial-shade tropical herb gardens. The rhizomes freeze and can be grated directly from frozen state for cooking, making storage simple.

common_name: Victoria Blue Salvia (Classic Dark-Blue Compact Mealy Sage for Gulf South Gardens)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting or plant container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Victoria Blue is one of the most reliable cut flowers for Gulf South summer arrangements — the flower spikes hold their color well for 7–10 days in water.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; Victoria Blue is the classic, standard-setting Salvia farinacea (mealy blue sage) cultivar — one of the most widely planted blue-flowering perennials for Gulf South gardens, producing dense spikes of deep violet-blue flowers with the white-powdery (mealy) stems that give the species its common name. The flower color of Victoria Blue is one of the most purely blue and deeply saturated available in a commonly grown summer perennial — the cobalt-blue to violet-blue spikes are more intensely colored than most Salvia farinacea varieties, providing a vivid blue accent in summer borders where true blue is difficult to achieve. Victoria Blue is seed-grown and therefore more variable than named vegetatively-propagated cultivars — but the best plants from a seed-grown flat are outstanding. In Gulf South zone 9, Victoria Blue grows as a short-lived perennial that often persists 2–4 years with occasional winter dieback and spring regrowth; it also performs as a long-lived annual from winter-grown transplants set out in spring. The long bloom season (spring through frost), excellent heat and drought tolerance, and outstanding cut-flower quality make Victoria Blue one of the most versatile blue annuals/perennials for Gulf South summer gardens. An excellent companion for Rudbeckia, Lantana, and Gulf muhly grass in summer and fall borders.

common_name: Stars and Stripes Cast Iron Plant (Yellow-Spotted Deep-Shade Aspidistra)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Stars and Stripes maintains best leaf coloration in good indirect light — too-deep shade reduces the yellow spotting intensity while direct sun causes leaf bleaching and burning.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Stars and Stripes is a variegated Aspidistra elatior cultivar with a distinctive speckled-and-streaked variegation pattern — small to medium yellow-gold spots and short streaks distributed across the dark green leaf surface create a starry, speckled appearance rather than the longitudinal stripe pattern of 'Variegata'. The spotting pattern is dense enough on good specimens to create a significant lightening of the overall leaf tone in mass plantings, brightening deep shade areas with the small yellow spots that gave the cultivar its name. Cast iron plant (Aspidistra elatior) is the standard-bearer for deep-shade ornamental groundcovers in Gulf South landscapes — routinely thriving under live oaks, Southern magnolias, and other dense-canopy trees where root competition, deep shade, and summer drought create hostile conditions for most other plants. The 'Stars and Stripes' variegation provides additional ornamental interest over the solid-green species while maintaining the same extraordinary shade and drought tolerance. The broad, strap-shaped, leathery leaves are deeply green with the scattered yellow speckle pattern that is attractive in both formal and informal shade garden settings. The small, inconspicuous, maroon flowers produced at soil level in late winter are characteristic but rarely noticed. Division of established clumps is slow — patience is required for new plantings to develop into full groundcover masses.

common_name: Green Shiso (Japanese Culinary Herb for Sushi and Tempura)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; green shiso self-sows prolifically once established — allow some plants to go to seed at the end of the season to ensure a volunteer crop the following year; or collect seeds for replanting.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Green Shiso (Perilla frutescens var. viridis) is the standard culinary form of Japanese perilla — the bright green, deeply toothed, wrinkled leaves with their distinctive aroma (a complex combination of mint, cinnamon, anise, and citrus that is unique and difficult to compare to other herbs) are an essential ingredient in Japanese cuisine. The large leaves (2–4 inches) are used as wrappers for grilled meats and fish, shredded as a garnish for sashimi and sushi, fried as tempura, pickled (shiso pickles), and used in a range of salads and rice dishes throughout Japanese cooking. The aroma and flavor are entirely distinct from red shiso (var. crispa) used primarily for coloring umeboshi and other preserved items — green shiso has a brighter, more herbal, slightly minty quality versus the more intensely aromatic and slightly sharper red shiso. Green shiso grows vigorously in Gulf South summer heat, reaching 2–3 feet and self-sowing prolifically at the end of the season. The ornamental quality is significant — the large, bright-green, textured leaves with purple tinges on the undersides and pink flower spikes in late summer are decorative in both herb and ornamental gardens. Regular harvest of the growing tips delays bolting and maintains leafy production through summer.
